title: Cross-Genre Constraint Index
description: Reuse map identifying shared constraint types, recurring scene obligations, and edge meaning usage across all 27 genre depth graphs. Generated from analysis of all genre graph.json deliverables and the controlled vocabulary in genre_edge_vocabulary.json and genre_node_roles.json.

constraint_type_index.summary: 16 recurring constraint types identified across 27 genre graphs. These are thematic patterns that appear as Core Constraint (Level 2) or Setting Rule (Level 4) nodes in multiple genres. Stakes Escalation is the most common, appearing in 8 genres. Every constraint type listed appears in at least 3 genres.

types.definition: Tension, danger, emotional pressure, or obstacles must increase through the narrative — the audience must feel that the situation is getting worse, harder, or more urgent before it can get better.

instances.label: Threat Must Be Present, Escalating, and Genuinely Dangerous
Threats Must Escalate to Match Heroic Capacity
Mental and Emotional Pressure Must Escalate
Emotional Escalation Justifies the Musical Break

types.definition: Speculative, magical, investigative, or threatening elements must operate according to consistent internal rules — they may be mysterious but they cannot be arbitrary or contradictory.

instances.label: Speculative Elements Must Be Internally Consistent
The Threat Must Have a Logic
Red Herrings Allowed, Cheating Is Not
Investigator's Method Must Be Visible and Consistent
Power Has Rules and Defined Limits

types.definition: Characters must face genuine moral choices, ethical dilemmas, or tests of their values arising from the genre's specific pressures — the story cannot avoid the moral dimension of its conflicts.

instances.label: Moral Ambiguity — The Line Is Blurred
Power Creates Moral Obligation That Must Be Tested
Personal Moral Code Substitutes for Law
Compromise and Strategy Are the Primary Means of Action

types.definition: The genre specifies the protagonist's relationship to the story's threats — either explicitly vulnerable (outmatched, isolated) or explicitly capable (skilled, resourceful) — establishing the genre's particular kind of tension.
The forces opposing the protagonist or the central goal must be real, consequential, and not easily dissolved — creating the friction that makes resolution meaningful.

instances.label: Obstacles to the Relationship Must Be Genuine and Consequential
Couple Must Be Mismatched or in Conflict Before Coming Together
A Central Question Must Drive the Narrative
The Case Must Be Genuinely Challenging
Clear External Goal Drives the Journey

types.definition: The genre imposes specific requirements on how stories can acceptably end — restricting the range of valid outcomes to those that fulfill the genre contract.

instances.label: The Relationship Outcome Must Feel Earned
Resolution Must Deliver Both Romantic and Comic Satisfaction
Resolution Trends Toward Connection or Understanding
Resolution Must Explain, Not Just Reveal
Clear Moral or Emotional Takeaway Without Preachiness

types.definition: Visible social structures, norms, or hierarchies constrain character behavior and generate conflict — characters must navigate, resist, or break these rules at personal cost.

instances.label: Social Rules Exist to Be Subverted
Social Hierarchy of the Period Constrains Characters
School / Social Hierarchy as Primary Arena
Family Rituals and Traditions Structure the Narrative

types.definition: Characters are cut off from external help — physically, socially, or institutionally — so that they must face the story's central challenge without a safety net.

instances.label: Limited Resources and Isolation Shape All Choices
Confined or Claustrophobic Spaces Reflect Internal State

types.definition: Characters must make meaningful, often irreversible choices that determine outcomes — the story cannot rely on things merely happening to passive characters.

instances.label: Choices Must Feel World-Defining from the Protagonist's Perspective
The Relationship Outcome Must Feel Earned
Character Interiority Is the Primary Engine

types.definition: Characters, events, and worlds must feel psychologically, historically, or scientifically plausible within the story's own framework — the audience must be able to believe in the world as presented.

instances.label: Characters Must Exist Within Their Time's Worldview
Based on a Real Person — Accuracy Matters

types.definition: Institutions — legal, military, political, professional — function as structural forces that shape what characters can do, constraining and enabling action through their specific rules and procedures.

instances.label: Chain of Command Constrains Individual Action
Historical Events Provide Structure and Stakes

types.definition: Characters maintain a gap between their public performance and private reality — the tension between these two faces is a structural source of drama, comedy, or revelation.

instances.label: Private Spaces vs. Public Performance Contrast
Public Versus Private Face of Power
Logical Absurdity — the Satirical World Follows Its Own Ridiculous Rules Consistently

types.definition: Actions — especially violent, criminal, or transgressive ones — must carry genuine, visible costs that the narrative registers rather than elides.

instances.label: Consequences Must Be Real and Proportional
Human Cost of Violence Must Be Visible
Period-Accurate Social Constraints Must Be Enforced

types.definition: The story must generate genuine wonder, awe, or the excitement of encountering the new — the audience's sense of possibility and delight is a core genre reward.

instances.label: Sense of Wonder Must Be Present
Discovery and Novelty Must Be Present
Imagination and Wonder Must Be Central
Story Must Be Driven by a Speculative Premise

types.definition: The central relationship(s) — romantic, familial, or interpersonal — must drive the plot as the primary story engine, not merely accompany external events.

instances.label: Central Relationship Must Be the Primary Story Engine
Romance and Humor Must Be Co-Equal Engines
Family Relationships Must Be the Primary Story Engine

types.definition: The physical environment is not mere backdrop but an active participant — a source of danger, opportunity, constraint, or narrative complication with its own agency.

scene_obligation_index.summary: 8 recurring scene obligation patterns identified across 27 genre graphs. These represent Level 5 (Scene Obligation) nodes that serve structurally similar functions in multiple genres. The Climactic Confrontation is the most common, appearing in 10 genres.

obligations.definition: The final scene where the story's central conflict is resolved through direct engagement — physical confrontation, psychological revelation, institutional reckoning, or decisive action under maximum pressure.
A mid-story scene or sequence where the central tension significantly intensifies — the situation gets demonstrably worse, harder, or more urgent before resolution is possible.

instances.label: Escalation of Absurdity to Breaking Point

obligations.definition: An early scene that establishes the genre's central challenge, question, or distinctive world — the moment the audience enters the genre's specific territory.
A post-climax scene showing the world after the central conflict has resolved — demonstrating that choices had lasting consequences and revealing the new normal.
A scene where a character makes a decision that cannot be undone — crossing a moral line, choosing one path over another, or committing to a course with permanent consequences.

instances.label: Clash Between Personal Desire and Period Constraint

obligations.definition: A scene where a major assumption is overturned — an ally revealed as enemy, a coalition fractured, or the protagonist's understanding fundamentally rewritten.
A climactic scene in which a character makes a public, costly, or irrevocable declaration — choosing love, truth, or principle in a way that cannot be unsaid.

instances.label: Public Speech and Political Persuasion Scene

obligations.definition: An early scene establishing both attraction and obstacle between the central characters — the first impression that will shape the entire narrative.

instances.label: Meet-Cute or Charged First Encounter Scene

edge_meaning_index.summary: 12 edge meanings defined in the controlled vocabulary; 7 are universal (all 27 genres), 5 have limited usage. 'specifies constraint', 'sets tone', 'branches into subtype', 'introduces setting rule', 'mandates element', 'requires payoff', and 'prohibits element' are the universal backbone of every genre graph.

meanings.note: Universal. Every genre graph uses this meaning for Level 1→2 transitions where the Genre Promise generates Core Constraints. Typically 4 instances per graph.
Universal. Every genre graph uses this meaning for the edge from Genre Promise to Tone Marker. Fantasy uses it twice (wonder as both constraint and tone). Typically 1 instance per graph.
Universal. Every genre graph uses this meaning for Level 2→3 transitions where Core Constraints generate Subgenre Patterns. Typically 4 instances per graph.

Universal. Every genre graph uses this meaning for Level 3→4 transitions where Subgenre Patterns generate Setting Rules. Typically 4 instances per graph, though some genres have more (War has 6, Young Adult has 6, Romance has 5).
Universal. Every genre graph uses this meaning for Level 4→5 transitions where Setting Rules generate Scene Obligations. Typically 3 instances per graph.
Universal. Every genre graph uses this meaning for connections between Scene Obligations and for edges that demand narrative delivery on established promises. Typically 2-3 instances per graph.
Universal. Every genre graph uses this meaning for edges leading to Anti-Pattern nodes. Typically 1-2 instances per graph.
Used in genres where the acceptable ending is constrained by the genre contract. Appears in Comedy, Romantic Comedy, Mystery, Family, and Children's Literature.

instances.label: Promise requires resolution delivering both romantic and comic satisfaction
Reveal scene must meet the standard of satisfying resolution
Promise requires resolution trending toward connection
Promise requires genuine moral or emotional insight without preachiness

meanings.note: Used in genres where the threat model must be calibrated to genre-specific requirements. Appears in Horror (2 instances) and Superhero (1 instance).

instances.label: Supernatural horror works through the uncanny
Cosmic threat is concealed by its incomprehensibility
Villain ecosystem calibrates and specializes the threat

meanings.note: Used only in Detective, where subgenre distinctions are drawn explicitly against sibling patterns. 3 instances.

instances.label: Classic puzzle focus differs from hardboiled moral danger
Institutional authority differs from outsider perspective

meanings.note: Used only in Crime, where the moral compromise is linked back to the crime scene through scope narrowing. 1 instance.

instances.label: Moral compromise often occurs at or because of the crime scene

meanings.note: Used only in Holiday, where the transformation constraint carries forward unchanged into the reconnection scene. 1 instance.

instances.label: Internal transformation enables the choice to reconnect

structural_observations: All 27 genre graphs share a consistent five-level spine: 1 Genre Promise (Level 1), 4 Core Constraints (Level 2), 4 Subgenre Patterns (Level 3), 3 Setting Rules (Level 4), and 3 Scene Obligations (Level 5), plus 1 Tone Marker and 1-2 Anti-Pattern nodes.

The standard graph size is 17 nodes and 16 edges. Drama, Romance, Romantic Comedy, and Young Adult have 18 nodes or 22 edges due to additional setting rule routing or extra anti-pattern nodes. Children's Literature, Satire, and Psychological have 15 nodes (fewer anti-patterns).

Seven edge meanings are universal across all 27 genres, forming the structural backbone: 'specifies constraint', 'sets tone', 'branches into subtype', 'introduces setting rule', 'mandates element', 'requires payoff', and 'prohibits element'. These map directly to the five-level spine plus tone and anti-pattern connections.

Five edge meanings have limited usage: 'restricts resolution' (5 genres), 'specializes threat' (2 genres), 'differentiates from' (1 genre), 'narrows scope' (1 genre), and 'inherits constraint' (1 genre). These represent genre-specific refinements beyond the universal backbone.

Stakes Escalation is the most pervasive constraint type (8 genres), reflecting that most story genres require a sense of increasing pressure — whether through physical danger, emotional intensity, or psychological deterioration.

Internal Consistency / Rules (7 genres) is the second most common constraint type, underscoring that audiences across genres demand predictable systems — whether those are magic rules, scientific premises, investigative methods, or threat logic.

The Climactic Confrontation scene obligation appears in 10 of 27 genres, making it the single most reused scene type. Even genres without an explicit confrontation node (Romance, Comedy, Family) have equivalent climactic scenes (Grand Gesture, Comic Climax, Reconciliation).

Genres cluster into natural families based on shared constraint profiles: (1) Danger genres (Action, Thriller, Horror, War, Western) share Stakes Escalation + Isolation + Environment as Active Force. (2) Investigation genres (Mystery, Detective, Crime) share Internal Consistency + Fair Clueing + Institutional Mechanics. (3) Relationship genres (Romance, Romantic Comedy, Family, Drama) share Relationship as Engine + Resolution Constraint + Public vs. Private Self. (4) World-building genres (Fantasy, Science Fiction, Historical, Adventure) share Plausibility + Wonder/Discovery + World Establishment scenes.

Six genres impose explicit Resolution Constraints — Comedy, Romantic Comedy, Family, Mystery, Romance, and Children's Literature — meaning these genres have specific, audience-enforced requirements for how stories must end. Other genres are more flexible about endings.

The Public vs. Private Self constraint appears across five genres spanning relationship stories (Romance, Romantic Comedy), institutional stories (Political, Biography), and structural comedy (Satire). This makes it a cross-family constraint that bridges otherwise distant genre clusters.

Every genre has at least one Anti-Pattern node. Six genres have two: Drama (Melodrama + Passive Protagonist), Biography (Wikipedia Page + Hagiographic Distortion), Young Adult (Condescension + Static Identity), Literary Fiction (Style Without Substance + Plotted Interiority), Musical (Narrative Halt + Inconsistent Song Logic), and Holiday (Cynicism Punished + Holiday as Backdrop).

Four of the 12 controlled edge vocabulary terms are used in only 1 genre each ('differentiates from', 'narrows scope', 'inherits constraint') or 2 genres ('specializes threat'). These may warrant future audit for expansion or retirement, paralleling the unused vocabulary terms noted in the archetype index.
